claim: Using language models to analyze conversations and extract high-level summaries of user intent without reading raw conversations preserves privacy while enabling analysis
actionable: Build privacy-preserving analysis systems by using LLMs to summarize conversations into intent clusters with numerical embeddings before any human review
source_title: What do people use AI models for?

claim: Clio (Claude insights and observations) uses three-step hierarchical clustering to identify user intent patterns: individual summaries → embedding-based grouping → model-generated cluster descriptions
actionable: To analyze large conversation datasets, implement: (1) LLM summaries of each conversation, (2) embedding-based clustering of summaries, (3) LLM description of cluster themes
source_title: What do people use AI models for?

claim: Privacy-preserving analysis requires defense-in-depth: minimum cluster sizes, aggregate statistics only, and automated scanning to remove identifiable information that could identify fewer than 1000 individuals
actionable: When analyzing user conversation data, set minimum distinct organization/conversation counts per cluster, remove raw identifiers, and scan for information identifiable to <1000 people
source_title: What do people use AI models for?

claim: Synthetic data reconstruction (generating test data with known distributions and validating Clio's accuracy in reconstructing them) is an effective way to benchmark clustering accuracy across attributes like language
actionable: Validate analysis tools by creating synthetic datasets with known class distributions (e.g., 10% math, 5% coding) and verifying the tool reconstructs those distributions accurately
source_title: What do people use AI models for?

claim: User research on AI systems shows significant unexpected use cases: research/brainstorming in scientific domains (quantum mechanics, materials science, biology), parenting advice, and subjective help (relationships, health, hair care)
actionable: When designing AI products, anticipate discovery of unexpected high-value use cases through post-deployment monitoring rather than pre-launch user research alone
source_title: What do people use AI models for?

claim: Task distributions differ significantly across languages: professional/academic writing assistance is more common in Spanish/Arabic; translation requests appear more frequently in non-English conversations
actionable: Build language-specific evaluations and features that account for varying task distributions rather than assuming all languages use AI systems identically
source_title: What do people use AI models for?

claim: Over-refusal (refusing benign requests like 'kill this process' in programming) can be detected and corrected by finding high-refusal-rate clusters, identifying over-refusals, and generating synthetic training data in those domains
actionable: Monitor refusal rates across usage clusters; when over-refusals are found, hire humans to generate training examples in those domains rather than modifying the model on real user data
source_title: What do people use AI models for?

claim: Under-refusal occurs when requests are technically misuse (e.g., translate harmful content to other languages) but phrased as benign tasks (translation); detection requires analyzing refusal patterns across linguistic contexts
actionable: For multilingual AI systems, analyze refusal rates by language and task type to find patterns where harmless-sounding requests (translation, rephrasing) mask potential misuse
source_title: What do people use AI models for?

claim: Coordinated abuse typically manifests as dense clusters of similar requests from many accounts with identical formats or topics, distinguishable from normal distributed usage patterns
actionable: Monitor usage clusters for unusual density and format similarity; dense balls of identical requests are strong signals for coordinated abuse versus natural user variation
source_title: What do people use AI models for?

claim: Users engage with AI systems in moments of personal crisis (emotional support, mental health, relationship advice) at significant volume; AI systems must be responsible in these contexts beyond policy violations
actionable: Build specialized safety evaluations for high-vulnerability contexts and ensure models provide sound, empathetic responses beyond basic refusal policies
source_title: What do people use AI models for?

claim: Bottom-up usage analysis complements top-down safety approaches; empirical data on actual usage can reveal blind spots in pre-formulated policies and safety frameworks
actionable: Combine pre-deployment safety evaluation (top-down) with post-deployment usage monitoring (bottom-up) to identify risks that theoretical frameworks miss
source_title: What do people use AI models for?

claim: AI models acknowledge their knowledge cutoff date and limitations inconsistently; real-world usage monitoring reveals this gap and allows targeted improvements via synthetic data generation
actionable: Monitor user feedback and usage patterns to find where models fail to acknowledge cutoff dates, then improve via synthetic training examples rather than real user data
source_title: What do people use AI models for?

claim: Users show significant emotional attachment to AI systems, including role-play and romantic discussion, representing unknown value judgments that AI systems must navigate responsibly
actionable: When building AI companions or assistants, design explicit guidelines for boundaries in emotional/romantic contexts and monitor real usage to guide appropriate responses
source_title: What do people use AI models for?

claim: Reproducible analysis methodologies with detailed prompts and hyperparameters enable other organizations to build their own versions and contribute to understanding AI usage across the ecosystem
actionable: When building proprietary analysis tools, publish complete methodology, prompts, and hyperparameters to enable reproduction and ecosystem-wide usage insights
source_title: What do people use AI models for?
